chapters.title: What does Christmas give the world?
chapters.text:
Two thousand years ago, an extraordinary event changed the world for good. Even people who do not follow any faith can see its mark, because it reshaped the way of life and the traditions of many generations.

The birth of the central figure of Christianity set the celebration in stone and shaped changes in religion, philosophy, culture, and politics.

Religious or not, try this question. Can you picture late December without the holiday? Wherever you live on the planet, you have heard of it and may even mark it with family and friends.

The season is a spell of magic that lets you believe in miracles, do good deeds, and help the dreams of others and of yourself come true. The holiday gives everyone the most precious thing of all, hope. That feeling reassures you that things will be fine, no matter how hard they look right now.

The most exciting part is that this holiday has pulled together people from every part of the world for thousands of years. Soon you will read about the beautiful year-end traditions of countries that share a lot. No matter what people eat at the festive table, what gifts they exchange, or how much fun they have, they know that many others around the world have gathered for the same reason.

No matter how old you are, where you are, or what you believe, well done on making it through the year. Now is the time to rest a little and get ready for the festivities. Turn on your favorite playlist, make some cocoa, and get set to travel through winter celebrations around the world.

chapters.title: Different countries read the holiday in their own way
chapters.text:
During the season, different countries put the spotlight on different parts of the celebration. In one Latin American country, for example, it is a time of acceptance and support. People remember a story about a young couple who asked townspeople for help so their child could be born safely.

Three wise visitors, who saw a higher power in the child and brought him gifts, shaped traditions in parts of Europe and the Americas strongly. By custom, they bring presents to children on the big day.

In parts of Europe, the celebrations start even earlier with a day for a famous gift-giver saint. The custom is popular in several countries. The story says that the saint leaves gifts under the pillow for obedient and kind children, and for adults too.

Remember the open-air markets? We owe them to one country in central Europe, where people have run them since the Middle Ages. Each year, the squares of big cities turn into places of music and fun, where visitors can drink mulled wine, ride the carousel, and buy gifts for family and friends.

Countdown calendars showed up in the same country in the middle of the nineteenth century. Before they became the sweet treat we know, they held religious verses and helped children count down the days.

In three Central American countries, the start of December is a chance to say goodbye to dark spirits that have moved into homes in the form of old, unwanted things. People burn the unwanted items, then celebrate with hopes for a good year to come.

A far northern country has carried another tradition since the Middle Ages. Each year in one city’s main square, the government declares a holiday peace, a promise that any crimes committed during this period will carry the harshest sentences. The declaration was not valid for the first time for a stretch of years in the early eighteenth century, when a neighboring empire occupied the land and broke the peace for nine years.

In three Middle Eastern countries, the communities that follow the religion of the holiday are smaller than the majority faith. Even so, at the start of December, people celebrate the memory of another saint. In honor of her courage and her fight for her faith, locals host dinners and plant sprouts that will be ready just in time for the big day.

For context, one European island nation learned the classic image of the holiday, with festive table, family, and decorations, thanks to a royal with roots in another country.

chapters.title: Pick the festive practice that fits you
chapters.text:
Now that we know more about the meaning of the holiday around the world, what do people actually do during the season?

A line from a book on the topic sums it up. Year-end celebrations around the world are as distinct, varied, and surprising as the people themselves.

A new custom was born in one island nation recently and is getting more attention each year. Locals swim in cold water. Not in the bathroom or the pool, but in the ocean. For them, it is a burst of adrenaline and a reason to feel extra joy.

Their neighbors would not understand that tradition, because many people spend the day before the big day in a steam room. For them, the steam room is a form of meditation and purification.

These days they also visit burial grounds where their family members rest. They light lamps and share family stories. In that way, locals start the new year by remembering the most important thing, the need to value human bonds and not forget those who are no longer with us.

In another far northern country, the holiday is a moment to grow national culture. Local books and collectibles are the most popular gifts among residents.

In many countries, the traditions carry traces of older celebrations that predate the modern religion. For example, in two far-apart parts of the world, people put on shows where they play as animals. In the past, such entertainment may have been linked to year-end celebrations, asking nature for a future harvest. In one Latin American country, there is a famous comic show about a bull that dies and comes back to life with the audience’s help. In a corner of western Europe, residents walk around town with a horse skull that talks, sings, tells stories, and asks residents to feed it.

A different scene plays out in a major Asian port city each year. Residents there do not try to revive old traditions. They invent new ones. From mid-December to the end of January, when the lunar new year starts, the city glows with thousands of festive lights. Holiday music plays everywhere, and new food carts appear each day. At some point, residents and visitors may even grow tired of the fireworks, because the city knows how to celebrate.

chapters.title: Every celebration has its own villain
chapters.text:
The joy of the season and the fun of receiving presents would not feel as sharp if nothing stood in the way. So we should also remember the partners and rivals who have reasons to spoil the holiday.

In parts of central Europe, tradition says that the gift-giving saint has a partner who punishes naughty children for bad behavior. Kids who know about this danger try to be polite, at least in December. The character has different names in different places. Though the figure looks like a demon stitched together from animal parts, few people truly fear him. He has become a familiar holiday figure, often shown on candy wrappers and greeting cards.

The villain story in one western European country takes a different shape. The tale of a man with a whip has little to do with the saint. He comes for the worst-behaved children, kidnaps them, and eats them. We can only guess that the sweetest children try not to upset anyone, eat their porridge, and keep their toys in order.

In one African country, the spirits of the season do not give gifts. They demand them. Actors in bright costumes visit locals, sing songs, dance, and ask for sweets or money.

People in many countries believe that the borders between the human world and the magical world break down during the year-end celebrations. That opening lets different creatures slip into our world to do good or bad things. The holiday is also a season of tests.

In one southern European country, people share a story about a tree that holds the heavens, the earth, and the underworld. Under the ground, goblins try to cut the tree down and split the world. But during the December celebrations, they rise to the surface to have fun and play pranks on people. When they go back to their work, they see that the tree has healed and still holds the three worlds. So the December miracle saves people and stops the goblins from tearing the earth apart.

chapters.title: Nativity scenes and straw goats take the world by storm
chapters.text:
An essential part of the holiday is the scenery. From the end of November, the world starts to switch on the lights, pick toys for the tree, and hunt for party costumes. Almost every country has a holiday decoration that will surprise you.

One central European country deserves the crown as the biggest fan of nativity scenes. In every city and many private homes, you can spot sculptures of the holy family, shepherds, and animals of all sizes. The locals try to build a full Bethlehem life with townspeople, musicians, and wandering vendors.

The scenery matters so much that officials have opened several museums dedicated to nativity scenes. The exhibits come in different sizes and materials, and some of them even move. Have you ever thought of a holy figure carved from sugar? Yes, this country knows how to surprise holiday lovers.

At the same time, one Nordic country has a soft spot for goat statues. Large straw sculptures show up in gardens and at the entrances of shops. People give each other small statuettes as souvenirs or to hang on the tree. Locals see the goat as a sign of respect for an older god of thunder, who ruled these lands before the modern faith. He harnessed two goats to his chariot.

The same country also kept a tradition similar to the Welsh one for a long time. A man dressed as a goat entertained people with jokes and songs and asked for a gift or a small donation.

In one town there, a huge straw goat appears on the city square each year. Residents and visitors came up with a strange tradition. They try to burn the statue, marking the end of the year.

In one eastern European country, a beloved home decoration is a bundle of wheat and field herbs called a special name. With it, locals show thanks for a good harvest and remember their roots and ancestors, as if inviting them to the holiday dinner. Often the bundle matters more than the tree. It sits in the center of the table as a sign of respect.

chapters.title: What do you do in your free holiday time?
chapters.text:
Beyond receiving gifts and spending time with family, the holidays finally let people relax after long work weeks. Alongside the usual activities, different nations have their own rituals that delight them and may surprise others. Let us see how people around the world have fun during the festive season.

A line from a book on the topic puts it well. Whatever your age, interests, or religious background, there is some year-end tradition that appeals to you. It is a season that offers something for everyone, even self-proclaimed grumps.

One city in a North American country has become the home of a contest for the best vegetable figurine. Yes, you read that right. Anyone can plant root vegetables in a special garden on the eve of the holidays. They wait for them to ripen, then carve them into festive figures. Nativity scenes are the most popular theme. Participants try to carve people, animals, and other symbols. Because each vegetable grows in its own shape, you will not find two matching figurines at the contest.

In one lowland country, people spend their free time writing funny poems about friends and relatives. Through the voice of the gift-giving saint, they describe the good and bad deeds of their circle. Later, they read the poems at dinner and decide whether each person deserves a gift.

In one African country, people love active fun. On the eve of the big day, all the men of the town gather to play a game that mixes soccer, baseball, and hockey. The game can run from morning to evening, until the players get hungry and head off to eat the festive meal.

In one southern European country, the holidays give people a chance to test their luck with a lottery. For some families, it is a tradition. They get together, buy a few tickets, and hope for a cash prize.

In family and friendship circles in one South American country, everyone loves word games. The rules are usually simple. Players must avoid certain words or certain answers. For example, everyone pairs up. During the evening, one partner avoids saying yes and the other avoids saying no. From the outside, it looks funny as the players trick each other into blurting out the forbidden word.

chapters.title: Last but not least, the festive dinner dishes
chapters.text:
Now we know what people celebrate, who brings the gifts, how they decorate their homes, and how they have fun in their free time. Where do they find the strength for all that? In other words, what do they cook for the holiday meal?

In one part of Europe, the festive dinner has twelve dishes, and meat is often missing. In three countries there, many people fast before the holidays. To ease back into their usual diet, they prepare a dinner full of vegetables, pastries, and grains. The number of dishes is symbolic, tied to the months and to the followers of the central figure of the faith.

In one northern region, the picture is different. A meat pie with potatoes, onions, and sauce is one of the main dishes. Many believe that settlers who arrived in the seventeenth century started the tradition.

In one South American country, cooking a traditional festive meal can become a weekend project. Each family member takes responsibility for one of the stages of making corn turnovers with a local name.

Someone has to handle the shopping. It is more complex than it sounds. The turnovers are made of pork or chicken, olives, vegetables, eggs, and nuts. Several people must prepare the dough, while others take care of the final step, boiling the turnovers. The finished dish looks like a wrapped gift. Each portion is wrapped in leaves and tied with a string. The turnovers keep for a long time, which lets people enjoy the dish even after the holidays.

In one Mediterranean country, the festive feast traditionally includes seven or twelve fish dishes. The meal is never complete, though, without pasta, wine, bread, salads, and the sweet cake with raisins and candied fruit.

The festive dinner in one East Asian country can surprise a lot of people. Those who call themselves followers of the faith spend the holiday at a famous fried chicken chain. Do not be shocked. The tradition came from a smart ad campaign in the mid-1970s. Back then, the chain had just entered the local market before the December holidays. In their ads, managers told the public that fried chicken is a worthy stand-in for the traditional festive bird. People liked the idea. Today they look forward to fried chicken holiday meals year after year.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Every nation finds something unique at the holiday. The season is more than a reason to gather for dinner and swap gifts. Some people use the time to thank loved ones for their support. Others call to share the stories of saints and their trials, or to invent new traditions that grow their national culture.

Even the strangest festive practices carry deep meaning once you know their roots. It helps to remember that what looks foreign or odd to us is not automatically wrong. The more we explore the meaning of these traditions for people from other countries or continents, the more open we become to the world.

Which story is your favorite, and which one surprised you most?

What feels familiar from childhood can carry different meanings for others. Stories from around the world wake up the inner child that wants to experiment and is not afraid of the unknown.

Now is the time to say goodbye. Thank you for making this trip around the world. Happy holidays, and may the spirit of the season be with you.

Try this

Share the stories you learn with friends or family. Maybe this year you will cook a new dish or set up a new evening of fun. Look at the traditions of other countries to understand their culture better.

Read it to the end?
